I'm planning to overhaul the module docstrings.  The basic plan is.

* write new clever parser/checker (including new features like SAMPLE OUTPUT)

* enable docstring rebuilding from parsed data so we can refresh docstrings to pristine states; indentation, section ordering etc

* `py3status modules list` etc to parse docstrings on the fly so it is up-to-date info and we can list all available modules including user custom ones, assuming we pass the include directories

* remove defaults as we can just get them from the module (we may need to add a `DEFAULTS` for 'special' settings in a couple of modules)

* because we have a 'structured' parsed docstring we can also make the modules documentation really nice and pretty

Anyhow that in the future.

We also need to unify config settings and part of this is gatekeeping new ones.  I don't think `api_key` is a problem here because that is what OWM call it.  But it is good to think about

This actually shouldn't be installed during testing. The first line (`pip install .`) installs the ChatterBot codebase for the current commit being tested. `pip install -r examples/django_app/requirements.txt` will install the current version of ChatterBot from PyPi.

Additionally, the tests also test on different versions of Django to ensure compatibility for each supported version. Installing from the example app requirements would alter the version of Django that gets installed to the test environment on Travis CI.

It corresponds to [the opposite operation](/apache/incubator-airflow/blob/b918a4976a641a3c944ef4ad4323fe25bd219ea0/airflow/operators/redshift_to_s3_operator.py#L96) (UNLOAD in RedshiftToS3Transfer), which outputs files named tablename + underscore + postfix that Redshift appends. For example, when RedshiftToS3Transfer unloads the table called foo, it will dump it to the files named foo_xxxx_part_xx. So `COPY schema.foo FROM 's3://bucket/prefix/foo_` loads these files to S3, since their paths are forwardly matched with the path specified in the FROM clause.

But I just noticed that in the case of using S3ToRedshiftTransfer solely, it's an unnecessary limitation that the input files must contain underscore in there name. And without that limitation, S3ToRedshiftTransfer works with the files dumped by RedshiftToS3Transfer. So I'll remove the underscore in the current PR.

Furthermore, these operators should be able to specify the table name and input file name independently. I'll file it as a new JIRA issue.

The file that matters is `requirements/default.txt` and `requirements/prod.txt`.
The file `requirements/constraints.txt`  is not important. If we accidentally have a package too many in that file (for example, something we forgot to clean up) it doesn't matter much because it doesn't get installed.

The only way to know if we have a package in `requirements/default.txt` that is no longer needed requires manual insight. One way to automate that is to perhaps run coverage.py when we start uwsgi and then run the smoke tests. Then you'd need to cross-reference that back to the package names.

So `install` step shouldn't be doing this work. Install is what gets run over an `sdist` bundle.

Stepping back, if we were to publish to pypi as we've been doing for years, we'd run `setup.py sdist upload`, which will create the `tar.gz`, and upload to pypi. Now, if we want to install, we can run `pip install ./dist/whatever.tar.gz` to install directly from the tarball.

This effectively extracts the tarball, and runs `setup.py install`. The problem is that we can't call `install_regexes` at this point since it's not a source. `install` already expects the artifacts to be bundled up.

I'm not convinced that we can actually support your use case without breaking the 90% use case of installing from pypi.

Unless we make `install` fail gracefully. That way it would work if the artifacts exist, and noop, or run `install_regexes` if they don't, for your case of trying to install directly from GitHub.

For now, I'm having us stay with the old semantics.

So in your revised example above, `out` is now scoped to the block of the entire for loop. Originally, `out` is just scoped to the `if/else` block. Since there's a `time.Sleep` call, `out` remains allocated for that entire time, and the GC can't clean it up.

The reason I did it the way I did is so that the GC can clean up `out` and that memory is not allocated for the entire time during this goroutine's inactive period.

Clearly, this is a micro-optimization, and realistically it is unlikely to ever make a difference in the Builder's performance. But my argument remains that it is preferable to code this way than to simply do what `golint` suggests.

How about something like this?

```python
    def read(self, entity=None, attrs=None, ignore=()):
        """Normalize the data received from the server.

        The server returns data in this format::

            {
                'id': 15,
                'name': 'name',
                'title': 'title',
                'subnet_id': null,
                'subnet_name': null,
                'operatingsystem_id': null,
                'operatingsystem_name': null,
                …
            }

        Normalize data to this format::

            {
                'id': 15,
                'name': 'name',
                'title': 'title',
                'subnet': {'id': …},
                'operatingsystem': {'id': …},
                …
            }

        """
        if attrs is None:
            attrs = self.read_json()

        # Choose fields to fiddle with, then loop through choices.
        field_names = [
            field_name
            for field_name, field
            in self.get_fields().items()
            if isinstance(field, entity_fields.OneToOneField)
        ]
        del field_names['ancestry']
        for field_name in field_names:
            field_value = attrs.pop(field_name + '_id')
            if field_value is None:
                attrs[field_name] = field_value
            else:
                attrs[field_name] = {'id': field_value}

        # `attrs` is now normalized. `super` can do its work.
        return super(HostGroup, self).read(entity, attrs, ignore)
```

I think there may be a problem here. This copies the vhost and ignores any other directives in the file. For example, a user may have:
```
<IfModule mod_macro.c>
    <VirtualHost *:80>
        ....
    </VirtualHost>
</IfModule>
```
When we copy the vhost, we remove these `<IfModule>` lines which seems incorrect. To be clear, this is the case for all directives that start a block not just `<IfModule>`.

One thing I'm not sure about is if directives contained in the block that contains the vhost should be copied. To provide a toy example because to be honest I'm not familiar enough with Apache directives to be aware of a real one:
```
<IfModule mod_magic.c>
    MagicSpell "foo"
    <VirtualHost *:80>
        ....
    </VirtualHost>
</IfModule>
```
Does `MagicSpell "foo"` apply to the Apache config globally or that specific block? If the former, we ideally shouldn't copy them but we probably should if it's the latter. Is it dependent on the directive?

I'm not sure how best to handle this. Perhaps we could copy everything in the highest level block that contains the vhost which shouldn't be too hard using our functions to manipulate Augeas paths and `span` but the two problems I see are:

1. Directives that apply globally we'd be duplicating. We've been doing this for years though by copying the entire file containing the vhost so maybe this isn't a problem?
2. There may be other vhosts in that block which we don't want to copy. We'd have to remove these somehow which will probably be a bit tricky.

I'm curious your thoughts on this.

    projects = (project for project in _projects(args) if _cloned(project))

Would have to make it something like this instead to get a tuple:

    projects = tuple(project for project in _projects(args) if _cloned(project))

In that case, the tuple constructor runs the generator to get the elements.

how about this? This generalizes the cases. Any content-length header is passed through and if none is found len(response.content) is added.

```
content_length_sent = False
for header_key, header_value in iteritems(response.headers):
    self.send_header(header_key, header_value)
    content_length_sent = content_length_sent or header_key.lower() == 'content-length'
if not content_length_sent:
    self.send_header('Content-Length', '%s' % len(response.content))
```

Hmm, but can we validly assume integer parameters to evaluate to integers in the CasADi/Python world?

```
parameter Integer n = 2;
parameter Integer m = 2 * n +1;
```

clearly `m` is also an integer, but it will be a casadi expression, thereby evaluating to an (integer valued) float.
